rationale: The citing paper uses Aggarwal and Min (2015) as a key prior account that links height to higher-level construal, and this motivates its own tests of construal as a possible mechanism. It then reports that its elevation manipulation does not produce the same construal shift and argues that construal does not mediate the elevation-to-risk effect in this context. The paper further positions its findings as a qualification of prior height-construal results by suggesting stimulus and processing conditions under which the effect may or may not emerge.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on the cited paper's height-to-construal idea but narrows it by showing that, in its elevated-viewpoint risk-taking paradigm, construal does not mediate the effect and instead operates as a boundary condition. This qualifies when prior height-construal logic applies.
